name: Classic Novels and Their Authors
description: Identify the authors of major works of literature from around the world

questions.question: Who wrote 'Pride and Prejudice'?
questions.explanation: Published in 1813, Pride and Prejudice is a Regency-era romance following Elizabeth Bennet as she overcomes her prejudice against the proud Mr. Darcy amid the marriage pressures of early 19th-century Hertfordshire, England. Originally titled First Impressions and drafted when Austen was just 20-21, the novel opens with one of literature's most quoted lines: 'It is a truth universally acknowledged, that a single man in possession of a good fortune, must be in want of a wife.' It has sold over 20 million copies and inspired landmark adaptations including the 1995 BBC miniseries starring Colin Firth and the 2005 film with Keira Knightley. Austen sold the copyright for just 110 pounds. The manuscript sat unpublished for over 15 years before finally appearing in print, making it her third novel written but second published.

questions.question: Who wrote 'Moby-Dick'?
questions.explanation: Published in 1851, Moby-Dick is an epic adventure novel in which sailor Ishmael narrates Captain Ahab's obsessive quest for vengeance against the white sperm whale that severed his leg, set aboard the whaling ship Pequod on a voyage from Nantucket across the Atlantic, around Africa, and into the Pacific. Its opening line 'Call me Ishmael' is among the most famous in world literature. The novel was a commercial failure during Melville's lifetime and went out of print by his death in 1891, only achieving its status as a Great American Novel after the 1919 centennial of Melville's birth. Originally titled The Whale in its British edition, the American publisher changed it to Moby-Dick at the last minute. The British edition omitted the epilogue explaining Ishmael's survival, bewildering reviewers who wondered how a drowned narrator could tell the story. William Faulkner said he wished he had written it, and D.H. Lawrence called it 'the greatest book of the sea ever written.' The 1956 John Huston film remains the best-known adaptation.

questions.question: Who wrote 'The Great Gatsby'?
questions.explanation: Published on April 10, 1925, The Great Gatsby is a modernist tragedy set during the summer of 1922 on Long Island, New York, in which narrator Nick Carraway chronicles the mysterious millionaire Jay Gatsby's doomed obsession with reuniting with his former lover Daisy Buchanan, while her brutish husband Tom serves as antagonist. The novel's famous closing image of the green light across the bay has become an enduring symbol of the American Dream. It sold only about 20,000 copies by October 1925 and was considered a commercial failure, but its reputation soared during WWII when 155,000 free copies were distributed to American soldiers. Fitzgerald partly based Gatsby on his enigmatic neighbor Max Gerlach, a bootlegger who frequently used the phrase 'old sport.' Notable adaptations include the 2013 Baz Luhrmann film starring Leonardo DiCaprio. The novel's copyright expired on January 1, 2021, entering the public domain and unleashing a flood of altered reprints.

questions.question: Who wrote '1984'?
questions.explanation: Published on June 8, 1949, Nineteen Eighty-Four is a dystopian novel set in Airstrip One (formerly Great Britain), a province of the totalitarian superstate Oceania, where protagonist Winston Smith attempts rebellion against the omnipresent Party and its figurehead Big Brother, only to be captured, tortured, and broken. Its opening line reads 'It was a bright cold day in April, and the clocks were striking thirteen,' and it closes devastatingly with 'He loved Big Brother.' Orwell wrote much of it while dying of tuberculosis on the remote Scottish island of Jura, retyping the entire manuscript himself at roughly 4,000 words daily despite fever and bloody coughing fits. The terms 'Big Brother,' 'doublethink,' 'thoughtcrime,' and 'Orwellian' all entered English from this novel. The title went through several drafts including 1980 and 1982 before settling on 1984. The 1984 film adaptation starred John Hurt and Richard Burton in his final role. It ranks #13 on the Modern Library editors' list of the 100 Best Novels.

questions.question: Who wrote 'To Kill a Mockingbird'?
questions.explanation: Published on July 11, 1960, To Kill a Mockingbird is a Southern Gothic bildungsroman set in the fictional town of Maycomb, Alabama during 1933-1935, in which young Scout Finch narrates her father Atticus's defense of Tom Robinson, a Black man falsely accused of rape, while she and her brother Jem encounter the reclusive Boo Radley. It won the Pulitzer Prize in 1961 and has sold over 30 million copies in more than 40 languages, never going out of print. Lee was ten years old in 1936 when real events near her hometown of Monroeville inspired the plot, and her childhood friend Truman Capote served as the model for the character Dill. The 1962 film directed by Robert Mulligan won Gregory Peck an Academy Award for Best Actor. The Library of Congress ranked it fourth among books 'most often cited as making a difference.' Lee published no other novel until the controversial Go Set a Watchman appeared in 2015, revealed to be an earlier draft rather than a true sequel.

questions.question: Who wrote 'Crime and Punishment'?
questions.explanation: Published in 1866 after serialization in The Russian Messenger, Crime and Punishment is a psychological novel set in 1860s St. Petersburg in which impoverished former law student Rodion Raskolnikov murders a pawnbroker based on a rationalist ideology justifying the act, then suffers agonizing guilt while the shrewd detective Porfiry Petrovich closes in through psychological manipulation. Considered the first great novel of Dostoevsky's mature period and one of the greatest works of world literature, it profoundly influenced psychoanalysis and existentialism. Dostoevsky burned his initial manuscript and rewrote the entire novel, shifting from first-person to third-person narrative. He wrote it under extreme financial pressure after losing his savings gambling, and simultaneously fulfilled a contract to finish The Gambler. He dictated the final chapters to stenographer Anna Grigoryevna Snitkina, who became his second wife. Notable adaptations include films starring Peter Lorre (1935) and John Simm in a 2002 BBC series.

questions.question: Who wrote 'War and Peace'?
questions.explanation: Published 1865-1869, War and Peace is a historical epic following five aristocratic Russian families through the Napoleonic Wars of 1805-1820, centered on Pierre Bezukhov's search for moral meaning against the backdrop of Napoleon's invasion of Russia. At approximately 580,000 words with over 580 characters including 160 real historical persons, it is one of the longest novels in world literature. Tolstoy's wife Sophia hand-copied the entire manuscript up to seven times as he revised it. The novel famously opens in French at a St. Petersburg salon, and roughly 2% of the original text was in French. The 1966-67 Soviet film by Sergei Bondarchuk won the Academy Award for Best Foreign Language Film, while the 2016 BBC series starring Paul Dano brought it to new audiences. Tolstoy himself resisted calling it a novel, insisting it was 'not a novel, still less an epic poem, still less a historical chronicle.' It was serialized before publication as a complete work.

questions.question: Who wrote 'Don Quixote'?
questions.explanation: Published in 1605 (Part One) and 1615 (Part Two), Don Quixote is widely considered the first modern novel and was voted the greatest work of fiction ever written in a 2002 poll of authors from 54 countries by the Norwegian Nobel Institute. Set in La Mancha, Spain, it follows Alonso Quixano, a hidalgo who loses his mind reading chivalric romances and reinvents himself as the knight-errant Don Quixote, embarking on absurd adventures with his faithful squire Sancho Panza. It opens: 'In a village of La Mancha, whose name I do not wish to recall, there lived not long ago one of those gentlemen with a lance in the lance-rack.' The novel has sold over 500 million copies and was translated into multiple languages within seven years. A spurious Part Two by the mysterious author Avellaneda appeared in 1614, which motivated Cervantes to rush his genuine Part Two to publication in 1615, just a year before his death. Cervantes and Shakespeare both died on April 23, 1616, though on different calendars.

questions.question: Who wrote 'Jane Eyre'?
questions.explanation: Published on October 19, 1847 under the pen name Currer Bell, Jane Eyre is a Gothic bildungsroman set in northern England during the reign of George III, following an orphaned girl who endures abuse at the charity school Lowood before becoming governess at Thornfield Hall, where she falls in love with the brooding Mr. Rochester while his mad first wife Bertha Mason is secretly locked in the attic. It was an immediate bestseller and critical sensation, though reviewers hotly debated whether a woman could have written its 'masculine' style. The character Helen Burns was based on Charlotte's real sister Maria, who died of tuberculosis at age 11 at Cowan Bridge school, the model for Lowood. The novel's most famous line is its triumphant closing declaration: 'Reader, I married him.' It ranked #10 in the BBC's 2003 Big Read survey. Notable adaptations include the 2011 Cary Fukunaga film starring Mia Wasikowska and Michael Fassbender. The novel revolutionized prose fiction as one of the first works to focus on its protagonist's moral and spiritual development through intimate first-person narrative.

questions.question: Who wrote 'Wuthering Heights'?
questions.explanation: Published in 1847 under the pen name Ellis Bell, Wuthering Heights is Emily Bronte's only novel, a Gothic tragedy set on the West Yorkshire moors spanning the 1770s to 1801, in which the orphan Heathcliff's consuming love for Catherine Earnshaw and his subsequent revenge destroys two generations of two families. Initial reviews called it 'wild,' 'confused,' and 'revolting,' but it is now considered one of the greatest English novels ever written for its radical treatment of passion and violence. The novel was originally published in a three-volume set alongside Anne Bronte's Agnes Grey, with Wuthering Heights occupying the first two volumes. Its frame narrative structure, told through the outsider Lockwood and the servant Nelly Dean, was innovative for its time. The 1939 film starring Laurence Olivier and Merle Oberon remains the best-known adaptation, and Kate Bush's 1978 debut single 'Wuthering Heights' topped the UK charts. Emily Bronte died of tuberculosis just a year after publication at age 30, having never known the novel's eventual acclaim.

questions.question: Who is credited with writing 'The Odyssey'?
questions.explanation: Composed around the 8th-7th century BC, The Odyssey is an ancient Greek epic poem of approximately 12,109 lines in dactylic hexameter, recounting King Odysseus's ten-year journey home to Ithaca after the Trojan War while his wife Penelope fends off suitors and their son Telemachus searches for news of his father. The poem's antagonists include Poseidon and the rapacious suitors of Penelope. Beginning in medias res, it established narrative techniques still used today. Whether Homer was a single author or a collective tradition of oral poets remains one of literature's great debates. Roughly 9,200 of its lines use formulaic repetitions, a hallmark of oral composition that allowed illiterate bards to improvise during performances. A 2018 BBC Culture poll named it literature's most enduring narrative. James Joyce's Ulysses (1922) is a modern retelling set in Dublin, and the Coen Brothers' O Brother, Where Art Thou? (2000) transplanted the story to Depression-era Mississippi. The word 'odyssey' itself entered English from this poem, meaning any long, wandering journey.

questions.question: Who wrote 'Les Miserables'?
questions.explanation: Published on March 31, 1862, Les Miserables is an epic historical novel set in France from 1815 to the June Rebellion of 1832, following ex-convict Jean Valjean's quest for redemption while relentlessly pursued by the rigid Inspector Javert. At 655,478 words in the original French, over 25% consists of non-plot digressions and essays on topics from the Paris sewers to the Battle of Waterloo. Hugo was an instant international bestseller, and he reportedly sent his publisher a telegram containing only '?' to ask about sales, receiving '!' in reply. The 1980 musical by Alain Boublil and Claude-Michel Schonberg became one of the longest-running shows in West End history, and the 2012 film starring Hugh Jackman won three Academy Awards. Hugo himself had witnessed the real 1832 Paris uprising and participated in the 1848 revolution. The novel was partly inspired by a real bread thief Hugo observed being arrested while a mother and daughter watched helplessly. Valjean's death scene at Pere Lachaise Cemetery remains one of literature's most moving finales.

questions.question: Who wrote 'Anna Karenina'?
questions.explanation: Published in 1878 after serialization from 1875-1877, Anna Karenina is a realist tragedy set in 1870s Russia following a married noblewoman whose passionate affair with cavalry officer Count Vronsky scandalizes St. Petersburg society and leads to her destruction. Its opening line, 'Happy families are all alike; every unhappy family is unhappy in its own way,' is one of the most quoted in all literature. William Faulkner famously listed it three times when asked to name the three greatest novels ever written. Tolstoy himself considered it his first true novel, distinguishing it from War and Peace, yet he actively despised writing the final sections and finished reluctantly. Notable adaptations include films starring Greta Garbo (1935), Vivien Leigh (1948), and Keira Knightley (2012), plus a 1961 BBC production featuring a young Sean Connery. The novel parallels Anna's tragic arc with the spiritual journey of Levin, Tolstoy's autobiographical stand-in, creating a dual structure that was radical for its time.

questions.question: Who wrote 'The Brothers Karamazov'?
questions.explanation: Published in 1880 after serialization from 1879, The Brothers Karamazov is Dostoevsky's final novel, a philosophical epic set in a provincial Russian town in which the murder of the debauched patriarch Fyodor Pavlovich becomes the catalyst for exploring faith, doubt, and free will through his three sons: the saintly Alyosha, the intellectual nihilist Ivan, and the passionate Dmitri. Sigmund Freud called it 'the most magnificent novel ever written,' and Albert Einstein named it his favorite book. Ivan's dictum 'if there is no God, everything is lawful' became central to existentialist philosophy decades later. Dostoevsky's three-year-old son Alyosha died of epilepsy in 1878 during the novel's writing, and he named the protagonist after his deceased child. Dostoevsky himself died just three months after completing it, in January 1881. The novel has been adapted into a 1958 Richard Brooks film, a 2009 Russian television series, and even a 2013 Japanese drama transplanting the story to modern Tokyo. Wittgenstein and Heidegger both cited it as profoundly influential.

questions.question: Who wrote 'Ulysses'?
questions.explanation: Published in 1922, Ulysses is a landmark modernist novel set entirely on June 16, 1904 in Dublin, following Jewish advertisement canvasser Leopold Bloom, young artist Stephen Dedalus, and Bloom's wife Molly through a single day that parallels Homer's Odyssey. It was banned in the United States for obscenity until a landmark 1933 court ruling by Judge John M. Woolsey. June 16 is now celebrated worldwide as Bloomsday. The novel closes with Molly Bloom's famous unpunctuated soliloquy ending 'yes I said yes I will Yes.' Considered 'a demonstration and summation of the entire modernist movement,' it pioneered stream-of-consciousness technique to an unprecedented degree. The first edition contained over 2,000 errors, and subsequent editions often introduced new mistakes while attempting corrections. Joyce told Vladimir Nabokov around 1937 that using Homer's mythology was 'a whim' and called his collaboration with Stuart Gilbert explaining the mythological parallels 'a terrible mistake.' The novel's 18 episodes each employ a different literary style, from journalism to dramatic script to catechism.

questions.question: Who wrote 'Catch-22'?
questions.explanation: Published in 1961, Catch-22 is a satirical war novel set on the Mediterranean island of Pianosa during 1942-1944, following Captain John Yossarian, a B-25 bombardier who desperately tries to avoid flying more combat missions while Colonel Cathcart keeps raising the required number. The title's central paradox, that anyone crazy enough to want to fly dangerous missions is sane and therefore must fly, entered the English language as a term for any inescapable logical trap. Heller's original title was Catch-18, changed to avoid confusion with Leon Uris's Mila 18 published the same year. The novel opens with 'It was love at first sight. The first time Yossarian saw the chaplain he fell madly in love with him.' Though the hardcover initially flopped, the 1962 paperback edition at 75 cents sold 1.1 million copies. The 1970 Mike Nichols film starred Alan Arkin as Yossarian, and a 2019 Hulu miniseries was produced by George Clooney. Ranked #7 by the Modern Library panel and #11 on the BBC's Big Read, it remains the definitive anti-bureaucracy novel.

questions.question: Who wrote 'Brave New World'?
questions.explanation: Published in 1932, Brave New World is a dystopian science fiction novel set in London in the year AF 632 (AD 2540), depicting a World State where humans are genetically engineered, conditioned into social castes, and pacified with the drug soma, until John 'the Savage' is brought from a reservation and collides with this engineered paradise. Unlike Orwell's dystopia of pain, Huxley envisioned a dystopia of pleasure, where people are controlled through comfort rather than fear. The title comes from Shakespeare's The Tempest, where Miranda innocently exclaims about a 'brave new world,' used by Huxley with savage irony. Huxley wrote the entire novel in just four months while living in Sanary-sur-Mer, France. It ranks #5 on the Modern Library's 100 Best English Novels of the 20th century. Huxley and C.S. Lewis both died on November 22, 1963, the same day President Kennedy was assassinated, their deaths almost entirely overshadowed by the news. The primary antagonist Mustapha Mond, one of ten World Controllers, is among fiction's most articulate defenders of totalitarianism.

questions.question: Who wrote 'Lord of the Flies'?
questions.explanation: Published in 1954, Lord of the Flies is an allegorical novel set on an uninhabited island where a group of British schoolboys, stranded after a wartime plane crash, attempt self-governance under the rational Ralph but descend into savagery under the tyrannical Jack Merridew. The manuscript was originally titled Strangers from Within and rejected by 21 publishers, with one reader dismissing it as 'absurd and uninteresting... rubbish and dull,' before Faber and Faber accepted it. The title translates the Hebrew name Beelzebub, literally 'Lord of the Flies,' a name for the devil. Golding eventually won the 1983 Nobel Prize in Literature, largely on the strength of this debut. The 1963 Peter Brook film and 1990 Harry Hook film are the most notable adaptations. Stephen King named his fictional town Castle Rock after Jack's mountain stronghold in the novel. It ranks #41 on the Modern Library editors' list and was chosen by Time as one of the 100 best English-language novels since 1923. The book remains a staple of school curricula worldwide as an exploration of innate human savagery versus civilization.

questions.question: Who wrote 'The Catcher in the Rye'?
questions.explanation: Published in 1951, The Catcher in the Rye is a coming-of-age novel set in New York City in December 1949, narrated by Holden Caulfield, an expelled prep school student who wanders Manhattan railing against the 'phoniness' of the adult world while wrestling with grief and alienation. Its rambling opening line, 'If you really want to hear about it, the first thing you'll probably want to know is where I was born, and what my lousy childhood was like, and how my parents were occupied and all before they had me, and all that David Copperfield kind of crap,' established an entirely new voice in American fiction. The novel still sells about 250,000 copies annually. It was the most censored book in American schools between 1961 and 1982, challenged for profanity and sexual references. Notoriously, Mark David Chapman possessed a copy when arrested for assassinating John Lennon in 1980. Salinger famously refused all film adaptation offers, became a recluse, and never published another novel, though he reportedly continued writing until his death in 2010. It ranks #15 on the BBC's Big Read survey.

questions.question: Who wrote 'One Hundred Years of Solitude'?
questions.explanation: Published in 1967, One Hundred Years of Solitude is the defining work of magical realism, a family saga spanning seven generations of the Buendia family in the fictional Colombian town of Macondo, where miraculous events occur alongside political upheaval and cyclical patterns of solitude and self-destruction. Its famous opening line reads: 'Many years later, as he faced the firing squad, Colonel Aureliano Buendia was to remember that distant afternoon when his father took him to discover ice.' The novel has sold over 50 million copies in 46 languages and was central to Garcia Marquez winning the 1982 Nobel Prize in Literature. He reportedly conceived the novel while driving to Acapulco and turned his car around mid-journey to begin writing, spending 18 months completing it while his wife Mercedes managed the household finances. He pawned her hairdryer to mail the manuscript to a publisher. The final child born in the novel has a pig's tail, fulfilling a century-old family curse. A 2024 Netflix series, produced with the author's sons as executive producers, became the first authorized screen adaptation.

questions.question: Who wrote 'Beloved'?
questions.explanation: Published in 1987, Beloved is a literary fiction novel with Gothic and magical realist elements, set in Cincinnati, Ohio in 1873, in which formerly enslaved woman Sethe is haunted by the ghost of the daughter she killed to spare from slavery, a specter who returns in physical form as the mysterious young woman called Beloved. It won the Pulitzer Prize for Fiction in 1988 and was ranked by the New York Times as the best American fiction work published between 1981 and 2006. Morrison drew inspiration from the true story of Margaret Garner, an enslaved Kentucky woman who fled to Ohio in 1856 and attempted to kill her children rather than see them returned to slavery. The dedication reads 'Sixty Million and more,' referencing estimates of those who perished during the Middle Passage. Morrison won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1993, becoming the first African American woman to receive the honor. The 1998 film adaptation directed by Jonathan Demme starred Oprah Winfrey as Sethe. When the novel was controversially passed over for the National Book Award, 48 prominent Black writers signed an open letter protesting the omission.

questions.question: Who wrote 'Invisible Man' (1952)?
questions.explanation: Published in 1952, Invisible Man is a bildungsroman following an unnamed Black narrator from the segregated South through disillusionment in Harlem as he grapples with systemic racism, identity, and the experience of being socially invisible in 1920s-1940s America. It opens with the declaration 'I am an invisible man' and closes with the haunting question 'Who knows but that, on the lower frequencies, I speak for you?' Ellison became the first African American to win the National Book Award for Fiction in 1953, and Time called it 'the quintessential American picaresque of the 20th century.' It ranks 19th on the Modern Library's 100 Best English-Language Novels. Ellison began writing in a Vermont barn in 1945 while on sick leave from the Merchant Marine, and spent five years completing it. It was his only finished novel; he worked on a second for 40 years, but it remained incomplete at his death and was published posthumously as Juneteenth. Barack Obama reportedly modeled aspects of his 1995 memoir Dreams from My Father on this novel's narrative structure.

questions.question: Who wrote 'The Sound and the Fury'?
questions.explanation: Published in 1929, The Sound and the Fury is a modernist Southern Gothic novel set in Jefferson, Mississippi, depicting the decline of the once-aristocratic Compson family through four fragmented perspectives spanning 1910-1928, centered on the absent Caddy Compson's transgression and its rippling consequences. The title comes from Macbeth's soliloquy: 'a tale told by an idiot, full of sound and fury, signifying nothing,' and the first section is narrated by Benjy, a man with an intellectual disability, on April 7, 1928. Its innovative stream-of-consciousness technique and non-linear chronology made it one of the most complex novels in American literary history and was instrumental in Faulkner winning the 1949 Nobel Prize in Literature. The opening line reads: 'Through the fence, between the curling flower spaces, I could see them hitting.' Faulkner wrote a companion appendix in 1945 without re-reading the novel, deliberately leaving numerous inconsistencies unreconciled. The 1959 film starred Yul Brynner, and a 2014 James Franco adaptation premiered at the Venice Film Festival. The novel entered the public domain on January 1, 2025.

questions.question: Who wrote 'Frankenstein'?
questions.explanation: Published anonymously in 1818 and under Mary Shelley's name in a revised 1831 edition, Frankenstein is a Gothic science fiction novel spanning Switzerland, Germany, England, Scotland, and the Arctic, in which young scientist Victor Frankenstein creates a sapient creature from body parts then abandons it, driving the lonely creation to revenge by murdering Victor's loved ones. Widely considered the first true science fiction novel, it originated during the 'Year Without a Summer' in 1816, when the eruption of Mount Tambora caused abnormal cold across Europe, and the 18-year-old Mary conceived the idea during a ghost story contest with Lord Byron and Percy Shelley at the Villa Diodati near Lake Geneva. The creature is never actually named in the novel; Victor calls it only 'wretch,' 'monster,' 'creature,' 'demon,' or 'fiend.' The 1931 Boris Karloff film created the iconic flat-headed, bolt-necked image that has defined the monster in popular culture ever since, though it bears no resemblance to Shelley's description. The Edison Studios 1910 film was the first adaptation. The BBC included it among its 100 most influential novels in 2019.

questions.question: Who wrote 'Dracula'?
questions.explanation: Published on May 26, 1897, Dracula is a Gothic horror novel told through letters, diary entries, and newspaper clippings, in which English solicitor Jonathan Harker travels to Transylvania and encounters Count Dracula, an ancient vampire who travels to England to spread the undead curse, forcing a band of heroes led by Professor Abraham Van Helsing to hunt and destroy him across late Victorian London and Whitby. Stoker never visited Transylvania; he researched the region at the British Museum and named his vampire after Vlad III Dracula, a 15th-century Wallachian prince. The novel was originally titled The Un-Dead before a last-minute change. Stoker failed to properly file U.S. copyright, accidentally placing the novel in the American public domain and preventing his descendants from collecting American royalties. Dracula holds the Guinness World Record as the most portrayed literary character in film history, with landmark adaptations including the unauthorized Nosferatu (1922), Bela Lugosi's iconic 1931 portrayal, Christopher Lee's 1958 Hammer Horror version, and Francis Ford Coppola's 1992 film with Gary Oldman.

questions.question: Who wrote 'Great Expectations'?
questions.explanation: Published as a serial in 1860-1861 and in book form in October 1861, Great Expectations is a bildungsroman set in Kent and London from roughly 1812-1846, following orphan Pip as he encounters an escaped convict in a graveyard, falls hopelessly in love with the cold Estella at the decaying Miss Havisham's mansion, receives mysterious wealth from an unknown benefactor, and discovers his patron is the very convict he aided years before. The novel opens with one of Dickens's most memorable introductions: 'My father's family name being Pirrip, and my Christian name Philip, my infant tongue could make of both names nothing longer or more explicit than Pip.' Dickens famously rewrote the ending after his friend Edward Bulwer-Lytton convinced him the original was too bleak; both endings survive and scholars still debate which is superior. During serialization it sold approximately 100,000 copies weekly. The 1946 David Lean film is considered the definitive adaptation. It ranked #17 in the BBC's 2003 Big Read poll and #4 among the 100 greatest British novels by BBC critics in 2015.

questions.question: Who wrote 'A Tale of Two Cities'?
questions.explanation: Published in 1859, A Tale of Two Cities is a historical novel set in London and Paris from 1775 to 1793 during the French Revolution, following the intertwined fates of French aristocrat Charles Darnay, the self-sacrificing English lawyer Sydney Carton, and the vengeful revolutionary Madame Defarge. It has sold over 200 million copies, making it possibly the best-selling novel of all time. Its opening, 'It was the best of times, it was the worst of times,' is universally recognized, and its closing line is equally famous: 'It is a far, far better thing that I do, than I have ever done; it is a far, far better rest that I go to than I have ever known.' The 1935 film was nominated for the Academy Award for Best Picture. Dickens was partly inspired by the play The Dead Heart by Watts Phillips, in which he had performed. Jorge Luis Borges quipped that Dickens could not truly write about two cities because he was fundamentally a London writer. A 2026 BBC/MGM+ series starring Kit Harington is the latest adaptation, proving the story's enduring appeal.

questions.question: Who wrote 'Oliver Twist'?
questions.explanation: Published as a serial from 1837-1839 and in book form in 1838, Oliver Twist is a social novel set in 1830s London following an orphan boy who escapes a workhouse, falls in with Fagin's gang of child pickpockets including the Artful Dodger, and endures the brutality of the criminal Bill Sikes before ultimately discovering his true parentage and finding redemption. It was one of the first English novels to center a child protagonist and served as a searing critique of the Poor Laws, child labor, and the treatment of orphans in industrial England. Dickens drew on his own childhood experience of working in a blacking factory at age 12. Fagin is referred to as 'the Jew' 274 times in the first 38 chapters; Dickens toned down this language in later editions after criticism from Jewish reader Eliza Davis. The 1948 David Lean film starred Alec Guinness as Fagin, and the 1968 musical film Oliver! won the Academy Award for Best Picture. George Cruikshank provided one original steel etching per month to illustrate each serialized installment.

questions.question: Who wrote 'Middlemarch'?
questions.explanation: Published in eight installments from 1871-1872, Middlemarch is a realist novel set in the fictional English Midlands town of Middlemarch from 1829 to 1832, weaving together multiple interconnected stories centered on idealistic Dorothea Brooke's disastrous marriage to the scholar Casaubon, ambitious Dr. Lydgate's medical reforms, and the corrupt banker Bulstrode's hidden past. George Eliot was the pen name of Mary Ann Evans, who used a male pseudonym to ensure her work was taken seriously. Virginia Woolf called Middlemarch 'one of the few English novels written for grown-up people.' The novel was originally two separate works -- one called 'Middlemarch' focusing on Lydgate and another titled 'Miss Brooke' focusing on Dorothea -- that Eliot merged in 1871. With the deaths of Dickens and Thackeray by 1870, Eliot was recognized as the greatest living English novelist at the time of its publication. The 1994 BBC television series was both a critical and commercial success. F.R. Leavis's influential 1948 reassessment established the lasting critical consensus of its excellence.

questions.question: Who wrote 'The Picture of Dorian Gray'?
questions.explanation: Published as a novella in Lippincott's Monthly Magazine in 1890 and expanded into a full novel in 1891, The Picture of Dorian Gray is Wilde's only novel, a philosophical Gothic tale set in Victorian London in which a beautiful young man wishes that his portrait would age instead of him, then pursues a hedonistic life of corruption under the influence of the cynical Lord Henry Wotton while his hidden portrait grotesquely records every moral transgression. Magazine editor J.M. Stoddart deleted approximately 500 words addressing homosexuality before the 1890 publication without Wilde's knowledge. The book was used against Wilde at his 1895 trial for 'gross indecency,' with prosecutors citing its homoerotic passages as evidence. Wilde himself said the novel contained three versions of himself: 'Basil Hallward is what I think I am; Lord Henry, what the world thinks me; Dorian is what I would like to be.' The 1945 film won an Academy Award for cinematography, and it has been adapted numerous times including the 2014-2016 TV series Penny Dreadful. The novel's climax, in which Dorian stabs the portrait and is found as a withered corpse while the painting returns to its original beauty, is one of horror fiction's most iconic endings.

questions.question: Who wrote 'The Grapes of Wrath'?
questions.explanation: Published in 1939, The Grapes of Wrath is an American realist novel following the Joad family, led by recently paroled Tom Joad, as they lose their Oklahoma farm during the Dust Bowl and migrate to California via Route 66, only to face exploitation and misery as migrant laborers. It won both the National Book Award and the Pulitzer Prize and was specifically cited by the Nobel committee when Steinbeck won the 1962 Nobel Prize in Literature. The title was suggested by Steinbeck's wife Carol, from Julia Ward Howe's 'Battle Hymn of the Republic.' The book was publicly burned in Steinbeck's home county of Monterey and banned in several states for its sympathetic portrayal of labor organizers. The 1940 John Ford film starring Henry Fonda as Tom Joad is considered one of the greatest American films. The novel's shocking final scene, in which Rose of Sharon nurses a starving man with her breast milk in a flooded barn, was too controversial for the film. Steinbeck's development drew partly from field notes compiled by Sanora Babb, whose own Dust Bowl novel was not published until 2004.

questions.question: Who wrote 'The Old Man and the Sea'?
questions.explanation: Published in 1952, The Old Man and the Sea is a novella set in the Gulf Stream near Cuba in September 1950, following the aging fisherman Santiago's three-day battle with a giant marlin, which he finally kills only to lose most of it to sharks on his journey back to shore. It opens: 'He was an old man who fished alone in a skiff in the Gulf Stream and he had gone eighty-four days now without taking a fish.' The entire manuscript was just 26,531 words, written in approximately six weeks. It was the only work specifically mentioned in Hemingway's 1954 Nobel Prize in Literature citation, and it won the 1953 Pulitzer Prize for Fiction. When Life magazine published the complete text in its September 1, 1952 issue, it sold a record 5.3 million copies in just two days. The 1958 film starred Spencer Tracy, though Hemingway disliked the result, and a 1999 animated short film by Aleksandr Petrov won the Academy Award for Best Animated Short. It was Hemingway's last major work of fiction published in his lifetime and restored his literary reputation after a series of critical disappointments.

questions.question: Who wrote 'A Farewell to Arms'?
questions.explanation: Published in 1929, A Farewell to Arms is a realist war novel set during the Italian campaign of World War I from 1915-1917, following American ambulance driver Lieutenant Frederic Henry's love affair with English nurse Catherine Barkley as they attempt to escape the war together, ending in devastating tragedy. It draws on Hemingway's real experience as a Red Cross ambulance driver wounded on the Italian front in 1918. A 2012 edition revealed that Hemingway wrote 47 different endings before settling on the final one, which closes with the stark line: 'After a while I went out and left the hospital and walked back to the hotel in the rain.' Called 'the premier American war novel from World War I,' it established Hemingway as a major American writer and made him financially independent. The novel could not be published in Italy until 1948 because the Fascist regime considered it insulting to the Armed Forces; translator Fernanda Pivano was arrested in Turin for preparing an illegal Italian version in 1943. Film adaptations appeared in 1932 and 1957.

questions.question: Who wrote 'On the Road'?
questions.explanation: Published in 1957, On the Road is the defining novel of the Beat Generation, a stream-of-consciousness narrative following narrator Sal Paradise and the charismatic Dean Moriarty on cross-country road trips across America and into Mexico from 1947-1950, seeking freedom, meaning, and spiritual transcendence in New York, Denver, San Francisco, and points between. Kerouac famously typed the first draft on a continuous 120-foot scroll of taped tracing paper in just three weeks in April 1951, though the published version underwent years of revision. The characters were based on real people: Dean Moriarty on Neal Cassady, and Sal Paradise on Kerouac himself. It opens with 'I first met Dean not long after my wife and I split up' and closes with a wistful meditation on Dean Moriarty. The original scroll manuscript sold at auction for $2.43 million in 2001, and in 2026 musician Zach Bryan purchased it for $12.1 million. The 2012 Walter Salles film starred Garrett Hedlund and Sam Riley. The novel inspired the 1960s counterculture and remains a touchstone for restless wanderers everywhere. Modern Library ranks it 55th among the 100 best English-language novels.

questions.question: Who wrote 'Slaughterhouse-Five'?
questions.explanation: Published in 1969, Slaughterhouse-Five is a postmodern anti-war science fiction novel following Billy Pilgrim, a fatalistic optometrist who becomes 'unstuck in time,' experiencing his life non-linearly from WWII-era Dresden to post-war America to the alien planet Tralfamadore. The novel draws directly on Vonnegut's own experience as a POW who survived the February 1945 Allied firebombing of Dresden because he was housed in an underground slaughterhouse meat locker -- Schlachthof-funf, or Slaughterhouse Five. Its opening line, 'All this happened, more or less,' was ranked #38 on the American Book Review's 100 Best First Lines. The phrase 'So it goes' appears exactly 106 times throughout the novel, used after every mention of death. Billy Pilgrim was partly modeled on Edward 'Joe' Crone, a fellow POW in Dresden, though Vonnegut kept this secret until 1995 after Crone's parents had died. The 1972 film won the Prix du Jury at Cannes. It was nominated for both the Nebula and Hugo Awards and appears on Time magazine's list of the 100 best English-language novels since 1923.

questions.question: Who wrote 'The Color Purple'?
questions.explanation: Published in 1982, The Color Purple is an epistolary novel set in rural Georgia during the early 1900s-1930s, in which a poor African American woman named Celie writes letters to God while enduring abuse from her stepfather Alphonso and later her husband Albert ('Mister'), eventually finding empowerment through her relationships, particularly with the fierce Shug Avery. Walker became the first Black woman to win the Pulitzer Prize for Fiction in 1983, and the novel also won the National Book Award the same year. It is classified as Southern Gothic, feminist, and womanist literature -- Walker herself coined the term 'womanism' as an alternative to feminism centered on Black women's experience. The 1985 Steven Spielberg film starring Whoopi Goldberg was nominated for 11 Academy Awards but controversially won none, sparking widespread protest. A Broadway musical adaptation opened in 2005, and a 2023 musical film followed. The novel has been frequently banned and challenged in American schools for its depictions of abuse and sexuality. It appears on both the BBC's Big Read and the BBC's 100 most influential novels lists.

questions.question: Who wrote 'Things Fall Apart'?
questions.explanation: Published in 1958, Things Fall Apart is a postcolonial novel set in the 1890s in the fictional Igbo village of Umuofia in colonial Nigeria, following the respected warrior Okonkwo as he watches his community fracture under the pressure of British colonialism and Christian missionaries, ultimately taking his own life when his people refuse to resist. With over 20 million copies sold in 57 languages, it is the most widely read book in modern African literature. The title comes from W.B. Yeats's 1919 poem 'The Second Coming,' not from Achebe's own words. The manuscript was nearly lost entirely -- it sat gathering dust in a London typing agency until a colleague retrieved it during her annual leave. The novel's devastating final paragraph, in which the District Commissioner plans to write about 'The Pacification of the Primitive Tribes of the Lower Niger,' is one of literature's most cutting ironic endings. Time included it on its list of the 100 Best English-language Novels from 1923-2005, the BBC named it among its 100 most influential novels, and Encyclopaedia Britannica listed it among the 12 Greatest Books Ever Written. A television adaptation by A24 with Idris Elba is in development.

questions.question: Who wrote 'Lolita'?
questions.explanation: Published in Paris in 1955 and in the United States in 1958, Lolita is a postmodern novel framed as the memoir of Humbert Humbert, a European literature professor who chronicles his obsession with and sexual abuse of his 12-year-old stepdaughter Dolores Haze, whom he privately calls 'Lolita,' across a desperate road trip through 1940s-1950s America, with the shadowy Clare Quilty as his rival. It was rejected by five major American publishers before the Olympia Press in Paris took it on. Nabokov composed the novel on index cards during butterfly-collecting trips in the American West, writing in English, his third language after Russian and French. The word 'nymphet,' coined by Nabokov for the novel, has since entered standard dictionaries. The opening line -- 'Lolita, light of my life, fire of my loins' -- is among the most famous in modern literature. Stanley Kubrick directed the 1962 film starring James Mason, and Adrian Lyne directed a 1997 version with Jeremy Irons. The novel has sold an estimated 50 million copies and appears on Time's 100 Best Novels, Le Monde's 100 Books of the Century, and the Modern Library's 100 Best Novels lists.

questions.question: Who wrote 'The Count of Monte Cristo'?
questions.explanation: Published as a serial from 1844-1846, The Count of Monte Cristo is an epic historical adventure novel set across France, Italy, and the Mediterranean from 1815-1839, following young sailor Edmond Dantes who is wrongfully imprisoned in the Chateau d'If for 14 years, escapes, discovers a vast treasure on the island of Monte Cristo, and orchestrates elaborate revenge against the three men who betrayed him: Danglars, Fernand Mondego, and Gerard de Villefort. At over 1,200 pages, it is one of the longest widely-read novels in Western literature. The plot was inspired by the real case of Pierre Picaud, a shoemaker falsely accused of espionage in 1807 who later sought revenge on his accusers. Its famous closing words are: 'All human wisdom is contained in these two words: Wait and Hope.' Cuban cigar rollers reportedly listened to the novel read aloud during work shifts, leading to the famous Montecristo cigar brand being named after it. Notable adaptations include a 1954 French film starring Jean Marais and a 2002 film starring Jim Caviezel. A 2024 French film and television adaptation brought the story to new audiences.

questions.question: Who wrote 'The Three Musketeers'?
questions.explanation: Published in 1844, serialized from March to July, The Three Musketeers is a historical adventure novel set in 1625-1628 Paris, following young Gascon d'Artagnan (based on the real Charles de Batz-Castelmore d'Artagnan) as he befriends the musketeers Athos, Porthos, and Aramis and becomes embroiled in court intrigue involving Queen Anne, Cardinal Richelieu, and the dangerous spy Milady de Winter. Dumas was so prolific that rivals accused him of running a 'fiction factory' with ghostwriters, most notably his collaborator Auguste Maquet. The novel's famous motto 'All for one and one for all' has endured for nearly two centuries. Israeli spy Eli Cohen used a French copy as a book cipher to communicate with Mossad, and that original copy is preserved in Mossad's museum. Volodymyr Zelenskyy, later president of Ukraine, played d'Artagnan in a 2004 Ukrainian gender-flipped musical where the three musketeers were women. Film adaptations span a century, from Douglas Fairbanks's 1921 silent classic to Richard Lester's beloved 1973-1974 two-part version to a 2023 French epic saga.

questions.question: Who wrote 'Adventures of Huckleberry Finn'?
questions.explanation: Published in December 1884 in the UK and February 1885 in the US, Adventures of Huckleberry Finn is a picaresque novel set along the Mississippi River in the 1840s, following 13-year-old Huck and the escaped slave Jim as they raft downriver seeking freedom while encountering con artists, feuding families, and the moral hypocrisy of antebellum Southern society. Hemingway declared 'All modern American literature comes from one book by Mark Twain called Huckleberry Finn,' and it pioneered the use of vernacular American English in major literature. Its opening line directly addresses readers of Tom Sawyer: 'You don't know about me, without you have read a book by the name of The Adventures of Tom Sawyer; but that ain't no matter.' The book has been both celebrated as a Great American Novel and repeatedly banned since publication for its liberal use of racial slurs. An anonymous engraver added an obscene detail to one illustration, and 30,000 copies were printed before the defacement was discovered. The manuscript's missing first half was found in 1991 in a steamer trunk. The 1985 Broadway musical Big River won seven Tony Awards.

questions.question: Who wrote 'The Adventures of Tom Sawyer'?
questions.explanation: Published on June 9, 1876, The Adventures of Tom Sawyer is a bildungsroman set in the fictional town of St. Petersburg, Missouri in the 1830s-1840s (based on Twain's real childhood in Hannibal), following the roughly 12-year-old Tom as he schemes his way through boyhood pranks, puppy love with Becky Thatcher, and genuine danger when he witnesses the villainous Injun Joe commit murder and later discovers buried treasure. The fence-painting scene, in which Tom tricks his friends into doing his whitewashing chore for him, has become a universal metaphor for manipulating others into doing your work. Twain named his protagonist after a real San Francisco fireman he met in 1863 who had rescued 90 passengers from a shipwreck. The novel was allegedly one of the first written on a typewriter, according to Twain himself. Originally a commercial failure, it became Twain's best-selling work during his lifetime. Notable adaptations include the 1938 Technicolor film and the 1973 musical film. The book reads as a nostalgic comedy but contains surprising darkness, including a murder trial and scenes in a cave with a dying villain.

questions.question: Who wrote 'The Scarlet Letter'?
questions.explanation: Published on March 16, 1850, The Scarlet Letter is a historical romance set in the Puritan Massachusetts Bay Colony from 1642-1649, in which Hester Prynne is forced to wear a scarlet 'A' for adultery while secretly bearing the child of the Reverend Arthur Dimmesdale, as her vengeful husband Roger Chillingworth plots psychological revenge. It was one of the first mass-produced books in America, and its first mechanized printing of 2,500 copies sold out within ten days, yet Hawthorne earned only $1,500 over fourteen years. Hawthorne was haunted by his ancestor Judge John Hathorne, who presided over the Salem witch trials without ever repenting; Nathaniel added the 'w' to his surname to distance himself from that legacy. D.H. Lawrence called it 'a perfect work of the American imagination.' The 1995 Demi Moore film took extreme liberties with the source material. The novel also inspired the 2010 teen comedy Easy A starring Emma Stone. Hawthorne himself doubted the novel's commercial prospects, telling his publisher it had 'powerfully written' portions but questioning whether it would sell.

questions.question: Who wrote 'Little Women'?
questions.explanation: Published in two volumes in 1868-1869, Little Women is a coming-of-age novel set in Massachusetts during the 1860s American Civil War, following the four March sisters -- practical Meg, tomboyish writer Jo, gentle Beth, and vain Amy -- as they navigate childhood to womanhood while their father serves as a Union Army chaplain. Alcott based the sisters on herself (Jo) and her real siblings, yet she resisted writing the book, confiding she 'could not write a girls' story knowing little about any but my sisters.' She completed the second volume in just three months, calling the whole work 'moral pap for the young,' yet it became her masterpiece. G.K. Chesterton believed Alcott 'anticipated realism by twenty or thirty years.' The novel ranked #18 in the BBC's 2003 Big Read survey. Notable film adaptations include versions starring Katharine Hepburn (1933), Winona Ryder (1994), and Saoirse Ronan in Greta Gerwig's 2019 film, which received six Oscar nominations including Best Picture. Jo March remains one of literature's most beloved female protagonists and an enduring feminist icon.

questions.question: Who wrote 'Tender Is the Night'?
questions.explanation: Published in 1934, Tender Is the Night is a modernist tragedy set on the French Riviera and across Europe during the Jazz Age, following the brilliant psychiatrist Dick Diver who marries his wealthy patient Nicole Warren, only to descend into alcoholism as their codependent marriage disintegrates. Fitzgerald drew heavily on his own marriage to Zelda and her institutionalization for schizophrenia. The title comes from John Keats's 'Ode to a Nightingale.' He worked on it for nine years and considered it his finest achievement, but it was his poorest seller, moving only 12,000 copies in three months compared to over 50,000 for his debut This Side of Paradise. Two different versions exist in print: the 1934 original uses flashbacks, while Malcolm Cowley's 1948 posthumous revision reorders events chronologically, creating an ongoing editorial controversy. The 1962 film starred Jason Robards and Jennifer Jones, and a 1985 BBC miniseries featured Peter Strauss. It ranks #28 on the Modern Library's 100 Best English-Language Novels, having gained the critical recognition Fitzgerald craved only after his death in 1940.

questions.question: Who wrote 'East of Eden'?
questions.explanation: Published in 1952, East of Eden is an epic family saga set in California's Salinas Valley from 1862 to 1918, retelling the biblical Cain and Abel story through two intertwined families, the Trasks and the Hamiltons, with the deeply evil Cathy Ames serving as antagonist after she abandons her husband Adam Trask to become a brothel madam. Steinbeck considered it his magnum opus, stating 'it has everything in it I have been able to learn about my craft or profession in all these years,' and called it the book he had been 'practicing to write' his whole life. The novel concludes with Adam's deathbed utterance of the Hebrew word 'timshel' -- meaning 'thou mayest' -- offering his son Cal the redemptive possibility of free will and moral choice. The 1955 Elia Kazan film starring James Dean in his first major role became a landmark of American cinema and launched Dean to stardom. Young John Steinbeck appears briefly as a minor character in his own novel. The first edition contained a notable printing error: 'bite' instead of 'bight' (a nautical knot term) on page 281. A Netflix limited series is currently in development.

questions.question: Who wrote 'Gone with the Wind'?
questions.explanation: Published in 1936, Gone with the Wind is a historical epic set in Clayton County and Atlanta, Georgia from 1861-1873, following the spoiled Southern belle Scarlett O'Hara as she navigates love, loss, and survival through the Civil War and Reconstruction, entangled with the rakish Rhett Butler. Mitchell won the 1937 Pulitzer Prize for her only novel, which she worked on for nearly ten years, writing the last chapter first and then working backward. It sold a million copies in six months and has over 30 million copies in print. A 2014 Harris poll ranked it the second favorite book of American readers. The title derives from Ernest Dowson's 1896 poem 'Non Sum Qualis Eram Bonae Sub Regno Cynarae,' not from Mitchell's invention. The 1939 film starring Vivien Leigh and Clark Gable won the Academy Award for Best Picture at the 12th Academy Awards and remains one of the highest-grossing films when adjusted for inflation. Its closing line, with Scarlett declaring 'Tomorrow is another day,' became one of cinema's most quoted phrases. Hattie McDaniel became the first African American to win an Oscar for her role as Mammy.

questions.question: Who is credited with writing 'The Iliad'?
questions.explanation: Composed around the 8th-7th century BC and first written down circa 522 BC, The Iliad is an epic poem of 15,693 lines in dactylic hexameter across 24 books, chronicling the wrath of Achilles during the final weeks of the ten-year Trojan War, culminating in his grief over the death of his companion Patroclus and the killing and desecration of the Trojan hero Hector. Despite covering a decade-long siege, the poem narrates only about 52 days, focusing on the personal rage and honor of its protagonist rather than military strategy. It opens with an invocation to the Muse to sing of 'the wrath of Achilles.' Homer was separated from his subject matter by approximately 400 years -- the Greek Dark Ages -- yet the Catalogue of Ships in Book 2 preserves remarkably accurate pre-Dorian invasion geography. Alexander the Great reportedly slept with a copy under his pillow. Considered one of the oldest extant works of literature still widely read, it is the foundational text of Western literature. The 2004 film Troy starring Brad Pitt as Achilles is the most prominent modern adaptation, though it takes significant liberties with Homer's text.

questions.question: Who wrote 'The Divine Comedy'?
questions.explanation: Written from approximately 1308-1321, The Divine Comedy is an Italian narrative epic poem of exactly 14,233 lines divided into 100 cantos using the terza rima rhyme scheme (ABA BCB CDC), following 35-year-old Dante on a journey through the three realms of the afterlife -- Inferno (Hell), Purgatorio (Purgatory), and Paradiso (Heaven) -- from Good Friday to Easter Wednesday in the year 1300, guided first by the Roman poet Virgil and then by his idealized beloved Beatrice. Dante populated Hell with his real political enemies, making the poem both a theological vision and a score-settling masterwork. Written in Italian rather than Latin, it helped establish Tuscan as the basis for modern standard Italian. Its most famous line, inscribed over the gates of Hell, is 'Abandon all hope, ye who enter here,' and it closes with 'the Love which moves the sun and the other stars.' No original manuscript in Dante's hand survives; approximately 800 copies from the 14th-15th centuries exist. Gustave Dore and Sandro Botticelli created celebrated illustrations, and the work profoundly influenced T.S. Eliot, James Joyce, and Samuel Beckett.

questions.question: Who wrote 'The Canterbury Tales'?
questions.explanation: Written from 1387-1400, The Canterbury Tales is a frame narrative collection in Middle English verse and prose, following a group of pilgrims traveling from the Tabard Inn in Southwark, London to the shrine of Thomas Becket at Canterbury Cathedral, who agree to a storytelling contest to pass the time. Chaucer planned 120 tales but completed only 24 before his death, and scholars still debate whether the work is unfinished or represents his complete vision. It opens with one of the most famous passages in English literature: 'Whan that Aprille with his shoures soote,' establishing the tradition of April as a literary season of renewal. No autograph manuscript survives -- all copies were made by scribes. The work is credited with popularizing English vernacular in mainstream literature over French and Latin, effectively making Chaucer the 'Father of English Literature.' He was the first poet buried in Westminster Abbey's Poets' Corner in 1400, establishing the tradition. Pier Paolo Pasolini directed a 1972 film adaptation, and a 1998-2000 animated series by Jonathan Myerson won a BAFTA and earned an Oscar nomination.

questions.question: Who wrote 'Paradise Lost'?
questions.explanation: Published in 1667 in ten books and revised to twelve in 1674, Paradise Lost is an epic poem in blank verse in which Satan and his fallen angels plot humanity's corruption after their defeat in Heaven, leading to the temptation of Adam and Eve and their expulsion from the Garden of Eden. Milton composed the entire 10,565-line work through dictation after going completely blind in 1652, using amanuenses and friends to transcribe his words. He was paid just 10 pounds for the first edition's rights. Satan's declaration 'Better to reign in Hell, than serve in Heaven' is among literature's most quoted lines, and William Blake controversially claimed Milton 'was of the Devil's party without knowing it,' arguing the poet inadvertently made Satan the most compelling character. The poem established blank verse as the standard form for English epics and explores profound themes of free will, divine justice, and the nature of evil. Its final lines offer consolation as Michael tells Adam of 'a paradise within thee, happier far.' Blake created extensive illustrations in 1808, and the work has influenced virtually every subsequent English-language poet from Wordsworth to Keats to T.S. Eliot.

questions.question: Who wrote 'Madame Bovary'?
questions.explanation: Published in 1857 after serialization in late 1856, Madame Bovary is a landmark of literary realism set in 1850s Normandy, France, following Emma Bovary, a provincial doctor's wife whose romantic illusions, shaped by sentimental novels, lead her into extramarital affairs and ruinous debt, culminating in her suicide by arsenic poisoning. Flaubert was prosecuted for obscenity upon publication but acquitted, and the scandal ironically boosted sales enormously. He spent five years writing it, sometimes laboring a week to perfect a single page, driven by his obsessive quest for 'le mot juste' -- the exactly right word. Called 'the most perfect book' from a narrative perspective, it elevated prose fiction to the status of poetry and prefigured modernist psychological realism. The novel ends with bitter irony as the corrupt pharmacist Homais receives the Legion of Honour. Notable film adaptations include Claude Chabrol's 1991 version starring Isabelle Huppert and a 2014 film with Mia Wasikowska. Eleanor Marx, daughter of Karl Marx, produced an 1886 English translation. When asked who Emma Bovary was based on, Flaubert reportedly replied 'Madame Bovary, c'est moi' -- 'Madame Bovary is me.'

questions.question: Who wrote 'The Stranger' (also known as 'The Outsider')?
questions.explanation: Published in 1942, The Stranger is a philosophical novel set in French Algeria, following Meursault, a French settler who shows emotional indifference after his mother's death, then senselessly kills an Arab man on a sun-drenched beach, and faces a trial where his lack of grief becomes the central focus rather than the murder itself. Its famous opening line, 'Mother died today. Or maybe yesterday; I can't be sure' (or 'Today, Maman died' in other translations), exemplifies Camus's philosophy of absurdism. It ranks #1 on Le Monde's 100 Books of the 20th Century and was voted #3 among the best French-language books of the 20th-21st centuries by 50 literary experts. Only 4,400 copies were printed in the first edition, with publishers using fake title variations to create the illusion of multiple editions. The transatlantic title difference -- The Stranger in America, The Outsider in Britain -- originated because publisher Hamish Hamilton feared confusion with a Polish novel. Camus won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1957 at age 44, one of the youngest recipients ever. Luchino Visconti directed a 1967 Italian film adaptation starring Marcello Mastroianni.

questions.question: Who wrote 'The Metamorphosis'?
questions.explanation: Published in 1915, The Metamorphosis is a modernist novella of existentialist absurdism set in early 20th-century Prague, opening with one of literature's most famous sentences: 'As Gregor Samsa awoke one morning from uneasy dreams he found himself transformed in his bed into a monstrous vermin.' The story follows traveling salesman Gregor as he struggles to adapt to his insect form while his family's initial horror gives way to resentment and indifference, ending in his lonely death. The German word 'Ungeziefer' has been translated into English more than 20 different ways, from 'gigantic insect' to 'gargantuan pest,' reflecting the deliberate ambiguity Kafka intended. Kafka explicitly forbade illustrators from depicting the creature, stating it 'is not even to be seen from a distance,' yet modern adaptations typically portray it as a cockroach contrary to his wishes. The novella has been widely analyzed through psychological, religious, and sociological lenses and is foundational to 20th-century modernist literature. Kafka asked his friend Max Brod to burn all his manuscripts after death, but Brod famously refused, preserving Kafka's entire legacy for posterity. The word 'Kafkaesque' entered English partly from this work's nightmarish premise.

questions.question: Who wrote 'The Trial'?
questions.explanation: Published posthumously in 1925, The Trial is an absurdist philosophical novel set in an unnamed Central European city, following bank clerk Josef K. who is arrested on the morning of his thirtieth birthday and prosecuted by an inaccessible, opaque authority for a crime that is never specified, ending with his execution and his final words: 'Like a dog!' The manuscript consisted of 161 loose notebook pages with unclear chapter ordering, and Max Brod assembled it 'to the best of his ability' after Kafka's death, meaning the definitive chapter sequence remains unknown. Kafka wrote the novel in 1914-1915 during World War I while working full-time as an insurance agent for the Workers' Accident Insurance Institute. It ranks #2 among the Best German Novels of the Twentieth Century and appears on Le Monde's 100 Books of the Century. Orson Welles directed a celebrated 1962 film adaptation starring Anthony Perkins as Josef K., and a 1993 version featured a Harold Pinter screenplay with Kyle MacLachlan. Philip Glass composed an opera adaptation in 2014. The word 'Kafkaesque' entered English to describe exactly this kind of nightmare bureaucracy where individuals are trapped by incomprehensible systems.

questions.question: Who wrote 'Notes from Underground'?
questions.explanation: Published in 1864 in the journal Epoch, Notes from Underground is a philosophical novella set in St. Petersburg, narrated by the unnamed Underground Man, a bitter, retired civil servant who delivers a venomous monologue rejecting rationalism and determinism before recounting his humiliating encounters with society, particularly his cruel relationship with the young prostitute Liza. Described as 'probably the most important single source of the modern dystopia,' it is widely considered the first existentialist work of literature, directly influencing Nietzsche, Sartre, Camus, and the entire existentialist philosophical tradition. The original Russian word 'podpolya' literally means 'under the floorboards,' not literally underground; Vladimir Nabokov argued the title should be 'Memoirs from a Mousehole' to better capture the crawl-space imagery. The novella challenged the utopian rationalism of Chernyshevsky's What Is to Be Done? and anticipated the anti-utopian tradition that would produce 1984 and Brave New World. It influenced works as diverse as Ellison's Invisible Man, Kafka's The Metamorphosis, and Scorsese's Taxi Driver. At just 100 pages, it packs a devastating philosophical punch that belies its brevity.

questions.question: Who wrote 'The Sun Also Rises'?
questions.explanation: Published in 1926, The Sun Also Rises is a modernist roman a clef set in 1920s Paris and Pamplona, Spain, following American journalist and expatriate Jake Barnes and the free-spirited Lady Brett Ashley as romantic entanglements and personal conflicts unfold among the Lost Generation against the backdrop of the San Fermin festival and its bullfights. Hemingway wrote the first draft in just six weeks while living in Paris, and the novel defined the disillusionment of post-WWI expatriates while establishing his influential minimalist prose style. It opens with the unexpected line 'Robert Cohn was once middleweight boxing champion of Princeton.' Hemingway strategically wrote the novella The Torrents of Spring to trigger a contractual termination with publisher Boni and Liveright, freeing him to move to Scribner's for this novel's publication. The bullfighter Romero was named after Pedro Romero, an 18th-century matador who killed thousands of bulls using the most difficult technique. The 1957 film starred Tyrone Power and Ava Gardner. The novel is recognized as Hemingway's greatest work by many critics and is considered an iconic text of literary modernism.

questions.question: Who wrote 'A Portrait of the Artist as a Young Man'?
questions.explanation: Published in 1916, A Portrait of the Artist as a Young Man is a Kunstlerroman (artist novel) set in 1890s Dublin, following Stephen Dedalus -- Joyce's fictional alter ego whose name alludes to the Greek craftsman Daedalus -- from childhood through religious devotion and sensual exploration to his ultimate rejection of Irish Catholic constraints in pursuit of artistic exile. The novel opens with a radically experimental child's-eye prose: 'Once upon a time and a very good time it was there was a moocow coming down along the road,' and builds to Stephen's famous declaration: 'I go to encounter for the millionth time the reality of experience and to forge in the smithy of my soul the uncreated conscience of my race.' In 1911, Joyce threw the manuscript into a fire in rage over publishing rejections, and his sister Eileen led a family rescue effort to save it from the flames. Ezra Pound championed its serialization in The Egoist magazine and arranged its American publication after British publishers rejected it. Joyce left Ireland at 22 and never lived there again, yet set all his major works in Dublin. The 1977 Joseph Strick film featured John Gielgud.

questions.question: Who wrote 'Mrs Dalloway'?
questions.explanation: Published in 1925, Mrs Dalloway is a modernist novel set over a single day in mid-June 1923 in post-WWI London, following 51-year-old society hostess Clarissa Dalloway as she prepares for an evening party, her stream of consciousness interwoven with the parallel tragic decline of Septimus Warren Smith, a shell-shocked war veteran who ultimately takes his own life. The novel opens with the famous line 'Mrs. Dalloway said she would buy the flowers herself.' Woolf's original working title was The Hours, later used by Michael Cunningham for his 1998 Pulitzer Prize-winning novel and the 2002 film starring Meryl Streep, Julianne Moore, and Nicole Kidman. Woolf originally planned for Clarissa herself to commit suicide during the party; Septimus was added later as her psychological double, allowing the exploration of death without destroying the protagonist. The 1997 film directed by Marleen Gorris starred Vanessa Redgrave as Clarissa. Woolf pioneered the stream-of-consciousness technique in this novel, influencing an entire generation of modernist writers. It appears on Time magazine's 100 Best English-Language Novels list and entered the U.S. public domain on January 1, 2021.

questions.question: Who wrote 'To the Lighthouse'?
questions.explanation: Published in 1927, To the Lighthouse is a modernist novel set on the Isle of Skye in Scotland's Hebrides from 1910-1920, structured in three parts around the Ramsay family's postponed and ultimately completed trip to a lighthouse, becoming a meditation on time, loss, memory, and perception as World War I passes in a devastating middle section called 'Time Passes.' Woolf based the Ramsay family on her own parents -- her father Leslie Stephen and mother Julia -- and wrote in her diary that finishing the novel freed her from the obsession with her mother that had haunted her since childhood. The novel's pioneering use of 'multiple focalisation,' shifting between characters' inner lives without traditional narrative, was revolutionary for its time. Her sister Vanessa Bell designed both the original dust jacket and the first edition's artistic elements. The autobiographical roots trace to Woolf's childhood visits to St Ives, Cornwall, where her brother Adrian was denied a trip to Godrevy Lighthouse, mirroring young James Ramsay's disappointment. It ranks #15 on the Modern Library's 100 Best English-Language Novels and was chosen by Time among its 100 best since 1923. The 1983 telefilm starred Kenneth Branagh in an early role.

questions.question: Who wrote 'Heart of Darkness'?
questions.explanation: Published as a serial in 1899 and in book form in 1902, Heart of Darkness is a novella set along the Congo River in the Congo Free State, in which sailor Charles Marlow recounts his assignment as a steamboat captain searching for the ivory trader Kurtz, who has established himself as a god-like figure among the local population deep in the African interior. Kurtz's famous dying words, 'The horror! The horror!' became one of literature's most quoted lines, and T.S. Eliot used 'Mistah Kurtz -- he dead' as the epigraph to his poem 'The Hollow Men.' Conrad based the novella on his own traumatic 1890 experience commanding a steamer on the Congo River. English was Conrad's third language after Polish and French, yet he became one of its greatest prose stylists. Harold Bloom noted it 'had been analysed more than any other work of literature' in university curricula. Francis Ford Coppola's Apocalypse Now (1979) transposed the story to the Vietnam War, with Marlon Brando as Kurtz, becoming one of cinema's greatest films. The video game Spec Ops: The Line (2012) also adapted its themes. It ranks #67 on the Modern Library's 100 Best English Novels.

questions.question: Who wrote 'Animal Farm'?
questions.explanation: Published on August 17, 1945, Animal Farm is a political satire in the form of a beast fable, set on Manor Farm near Willingdon, England, where the animals revolt against their human master only to have the revolution hijacked by the power-hungry pig Napoleon (representing Stalin), who establishes a dictatorship as oppressive as the original regime, aided by the propagandist pig Squealer and enforced by attack dogs. Its most famous line, 'All animals are equal, but some animals are more equal than others,' perfectly distills the corruption of revolutionary ideals. The manuscript was rejected by multiple publishers who feared offending the Soviet Union, then a wartime ally; T.S. Eliot personally turned it down as an editor at Faber and Faber. The manuscript nearly perished when a German V-1 flying bomb destroyed Orwell's London flat in June 1944, and he spent hours retrieving pages from the rubble. Between 1952 and 1957, the CIA's Operation Aedinosaur sent millions of balloon-delivered copies into Poland, Hungary, and Czechoslovakia as Cold War propaganda. The 1954 animated film was partly funded by the CIA. Orwell called it the first work in which he consciously fused political purpose with artistic intent.

questions.question: Who wrote 'This Side of Paradise'?
questions.explanation: Published in 1920, This Side of Paradise is a bildungsroman following handsome, ambitious Amory Blaine from his privileged upbringing in Minneapolis through Princeton University and into post-WWI disillusionment in New York, as he pursues success, romance, and self-awareness across the Jazz Age landscape. It was Fitzgerald's debut novel, written at age 23, and its initial print run of 3,000 copies sold out in just three days, making him an overnight celebrity and establishing him as the chronicler of Jazz Age youth. Its success convinced Zelda Sayre to marry him -- she had previously broken off their engagement, doubting his financial prospects. The novel sparked national debate about changing social morals among American youth, including drinking and sexual behavior. It closes with Amory's declaration: 'I know myself... but that is all.' Despite the novel's role in launching Fitzgerald's career, after his death in 1940 Princeton University's librarian rejected his widow's offer to sell his papers for $3,750, dismissing him as a mediocre author who had merely been 'lucky enough to attend Princeton.' Those papers would later become priceless literary treasures.

questions.question: Who wrote 'Treasure Island'?
questions.explanation: Published in 1883 after serialization from 1881-1882, Treasure Island is an adventure novel set in the mid-18th century, following young Jim Hawkins from a Devon inn to a Caribbean island as he joins a voyage to find buried pirate treasure, only to discover the ship's one-legged cook Long John Silver is leading a mutiny to seize the riches. Originally titled The Sea Cook: A Story for Boys and published under the pseudonym Captain George North, it was created to amuse Stevenson's 12-year-old stepson during bad weather in Scotland, inspired by a treasure map Stevenson drew first before writing the story to accompany it. The novel established virtually every enduring pirate fiction trope: treasure maps marked with 'X,' peg-legged sailors with parrots on their shoulders, tropical islands, and the Black Spot as a pirate death sentence. The 1950 Disney film was the studio's first entirely live-action feature, starring Bobby Driscoll as Jim. The 1996 Muppet Treasure Island is a beloved comic adaptation. The 2014 Starz series Black Sails served as a prequel. It was Stevenson's first major commercial and critical success, launching his career as one of the most popular writers of the Victorian era.

questions.question: Who wrote 'Strange Case of Dr Jekyll and Mr Hyde'?
questions.explanation: Published in 1886, Strange Case of Dr Jekyll and Mr Hyde is a Gothic horror novella set in Victorian London, in which lawyer Gabriel Utterson investigates the mysterious connection between his respectable friend Dr. Henry Jekyll and the violent criminal Edward Hyde, uncovering the horrifying truth that they are the same person, transformed by a chemical potion that separates Jekyll's good and evil natures. The phrase 'Jekyll and Hyde' entered the English language to describe anyone with dual good-and-evil personalities. Stevenson reportedly wrote the first draft in three days during a fever-induced nightmare, burned it on his wife's criticism, then rewrote it in another three days. The 1888 London stage production starring Richard Mansfield was forced to close after only ten weeks because Mansfield's transformation was so convincing that he became a genuine suspect in the Jack the Ripper murders. The story has been adapted into over 120 films, making it one of the most filmed literary works in history, with notable versions in 1920, 1931, and the 1990 Frank Wildhorn musical. Stevenson wrote 'Jekyll' to be pronounced 'Jee-kyll' with a long 'e,' though the short 'e' pronunciation has become standard.

questions.question: Who wrote 'Gulliver's Travels'?
questions.explanation: Published in 1726, Gulliver's Travels is a satirical prose novel following ship's surgeon Lemuel Gulliver through four fantastical voyages: to Lilliput (land of tiny people), Brobdingnag (land of giants), the flying island of Laputa (impractical intellectuals), and the land of the Houyhnhnms (rational horses who rule over the brutish human-like Yahoos). Though often bowdlerized into a children's adventure, it is a savage political satire targeting human nature, colonialism, and the scientific establishment of Swift's era. The word 'Lilliputian' (meaning small or trivial) and 'Yahoo' (meaning a brute or uncouth person) both entered English from this novel. In computer science, the terms 'big-endian' and 'little-endian' derive from the book's satirical dispute about which end of an egg to crack. Publisher Benjamin Motte secretly used five printing houses simultaneously to prevent piracy when the first edition was released. It was, as John Gay wrote, 'universally read, from the cabinet council to the nursery.' Notable adaptations include a 1996 TV miniseries starring Ted Danson. Swift published the book anonymously, presenting it as a genuine travelogue by Gulliver himself.

questions.question: Who wrote 'Robinson Crusoe'?
questions.explanation: Published on April 25, 1719, Robinson Crusoe is often called the first English novel, an adventure story following Robinson Crusoe (born Robinson Kreutznaer), an English sailor from York who is shipwrecked on a remote Caribbean island near Venezuela and spends 28 years there from 1651-1687, surviving alone until he encounters the native man he names Friday. Defoe based it partly on the real story of Alexander Selkirk, a Scottish sailor who lived alone on a Pacific island for over four years after being marooned in 1704. The first edition credited Robinson Crusoe as the actual author, and many readers believed it was a genuine nonfiction travelogue rather than fiction. The footprint discovery scene -- 'I was exceedingly surprised with the print of a man's naked foot on the shore' -- is one of literature's most iconic moments of terror and wonder.
