The book went through four editions before year's end and spawned an entire literary genre called the 'Robinsonade,' with over 700 derivative works by the 19th century. It influenced works from Swiss Family Robinson to Cast Away (2000). Jacques Offenbach composed an opera comique based on it in 1867. Defoe was nearly 60 years old when he wrote it.

questions.question: Who wrote 'Emma'?
questions.explanation: Published in December 1815 (with the title page dated 1816), Emma is a comedy of manners set in the fictional Surrey village of Highbury in early 19th-century Regency England, following the wealthy, meddlesome Emma Woodhouse as she plays matchmaker for her neighbors while remaining blind to her own feelings until she realizes she loves her longtime friend and neighbor Mr. Knightley. The novel's opening line brilliantly establishes its protagonist: 'Emma Woodhouse, handsome, clever, and rich, with a comfortable home and a happy disposition, seemed to unite some of the best blessings of existence.' Austen told a friend 'I am going to take a heroine whom no one but myself will much like,' yet Emma became hugely popular. The novel was dedicated to the Prince Regent, who kept a set of Austen's works in each of his residences. The 1995 film Clueless transplanted the story to Beverly Hills with Alicia Silverstone, becoming a defining 1990s teen movie. Other notable adaptations include the 1996 Gwyneth Paltrow film and the 2020 version starring Anya Taylor-Joy. Emma's innovation of narrating through the distorting lens of its protagonist's unreliable perspective was revolutionary for its time.

questions.question: Who wrote 'Sense and Sensibility'?
questions.explanation: Published in 1811, Sense and Sensibility is a Regency-era romance set in Sussex and Devon, England in the 1790s, following two sisters with contrasting temperaments -- rational Elinor (sense) and emotional Marianne (sensibility) -- as they navigate love, heartbreak, and social pressures after their family loses their estate following their father's death. It was Austen's first published novel, though she had written an early epistolary draft titled Elinor and Marianne sixteen years earlier. Published anonymously as being 'By a Lady,' Austen paid for publication herself and earned a profit of 140 pounds on 750 copies. The original French translation by Isabelle de Montolieu in 1815 was so heavily altered it essentially became a different story with fabricated emotional scenes. The 1995 Ang Lee film, with a screenplay by Emma Thompson who also starred as Elinor, won Thompson the Academy Award for Best Adapted Screenplay and earned the film seven nominations. The 2008 BBC television series and a 2024 Hallmark adaptation demonstrate the story's continuing relevance. The novel explores the tension between prudent restraint and passionate feeling, themes that remain central to romantic fiction.

questions.question: Who wrote 'Tess of the d'Urbervilles'?
questions.explanation: Published in 1891, Tess of the d'Urbervilles is a social tragedy set in Hardy's fictional Wessex (based on rural Dorset and surrounding counties) in the 1870s, following young country girl Tess Durbeyfield who is seduced and raped by the wealthy Alec d'Urberville, bears a child who dies, then marries the gentleman farmer Angel Clare who abandons her upon learning her past, driving her ultimately to murder Alec and face execution. Hardy controversially subtitled it 'A Pure Woman Faithfully Presented,' directly defending Tess's virtue and scandalizing Victorian society. The novel was initially serialized in censored form in The Graphic newspaper before appearing uncensored in book form. Hardy witnessed the hanging of Elizabeth Martha Brown at age 16 -- a woman who had killed her violent husband -- and the experience directly inspired the novel's tragic ending. The backlash against this novel and his next, Jude the Obscure, was so fierce that Hardy gave up fiction entirely and spent the rest of his career writing poetry. Roman Polanski directed a celebrated 1979 film adaptation starring Nastassja Kinski, and BBC television adaptations appeared in 1998 and 2008.

questions.question: Who wrote 'The Return of the Native'?
questions.explanation: Published in 1878, The Return of the Native is a Victorian novel set on Egdon Heath in Hardy's fictional Wessex (based on the heathlands near his Dorset birthplace), covering exactly one year and one day in which Clym Yeobright returns from Paris to his native heath to become a schoolmaster but becomes entangled in a destructive love quadrangle with the passionate Eustacia Vye, the steadfast Thomasin, and the mysterious reddleman Diggory Venn. Hardy considered the heath itself the novel's true protagonist, opening with a famous chapter devoted entirely to describing its brooding, primeval landscape. The novel demonstrates classical tragic structure with a five-act framework inspired by Greek drama, but Hardy originally added a sixth book with a happier ending for Diggory Venn and Thomasin due to serial publication pressures from the magazine editor, and he later suggested readers could choose their own preferred ending. The 1994 Hallmark Hall of Fame television film starred a young Catherine Zeta-Jones as Eustacia Vye. The novel faced publishing difficulty due to its controversial acknowledgment of illicit sexual relationships, shocking for Victorian readers. It became one of Hardy's most popular novels in the 20th century.

questions.question: Who wrote 'The Handmaid's Tale'?
questions.explanation: Published in 1985, The Handmaid's Tale is a dystopian speculative fiction novel set in the near-future Republic of Gilead (formerly the Boston and Cambridge area of New England), where a totalitarian theocracy has overthrown the U.S. government and forced fertile women like the protagonist Offred (whose real name may be June) into reproductive servitude as 'Handmaids' bearing children for elite couples. Atwood has said every atrocity in the novel has a real historical precedent, and she began writing it in West Berlin, within sight of the Berlin Wall. It won the 1985 Governor General's Award and the inaugural Arthur C. Clarke Award in 1987. The phrase 'Nolite te bastardes carborundorum' ('Don't let the bastards grind you down'), though mock-Latin, became a feminist rallying cry. The iconic handmaid bonnets were inspired by the Old Dutch Cleanser mascot, which frightened Atwood as a child. In 2022, Penguin Random House produced an 'unburnable' edition to protest book banning, which sold for $130,000 at auction. The 2017 Hulu television series starring Elisabeth Moss became a cultural phenomenon, and a 1990 film adaptation also exists. Atwood published a sequel, The Testaments, in 2019, which won the Booker Prize.

questions.question: Who wrote 'Cat's Cradle'?
questions.explanation: Published in 1963, Cat's Cradle is a satirical postmodern science fiction novel set partly in the fictional Caribbean island nation of San Lorenzo, following an unnamed narrator (who calls himself Jonah) as he investigates the creator of the atomic bomb and becomes entangled with the inventor's children, who possess a substance called ice-nine capable of freezing all water on Earth. Its opening line, 'Call me Jonah,' deliberately echoes Moby-Dick's 'Call me Ishmael.' Vonnegut submitted it as his master's thesis in anthropology at the University of Chicago, which initially rejected it; they finally accepted it in 1971 after he became famous, awarding him an M.A. The novel's 127 short chapters function as tiny mosaic tiles of dark humor, establishing Vonnegut's signature fragmentary style. The Grateful Dead named their publishing company Ice Nine after the novel's fictional apocalyptic substance. It explores Cold War anxieties, the moral responsibility of scientists, and the absurdity of religion through the invented faith of Bokononism, whose central tenet is that all of its own teachings are lies. The novel was banned in Strongsville, Ohio in 1972, though the ban was overturned in 1976.

questions.question: Who wrote 'Fahrenheit 451'?
questions.explanation: Published in 1953, Fahrenheit 451 is a dystopian science fiction novel set in an unspecified future American city where fireman Guy Montag's job is to burn books rather than put out fires, under the direction of his cynical, well-read captain Beatty, until Montag's encounter with a free-thinking young woman causes him to rebel and ultimately join a community of book-memorizing exiles. The title refers to the temperature at which paper supposedly auto-ignites. Bradbury wrote the first draft in just nine days on rented typewriters in the UCLA Powell Library basement, paying ten cents per half hour. The original 1953 hardback edition produced 200 signed copies bound in asbestos, creating a darkly ironic fireproof book about book-burning. The novel won the 1954 American Academy of Arts and Letters Award and the 1984 Prometheus Hall of Fame Award. Francois Truffaut directed the 1966 film, and HBO produced a 2018 television film. Bradbury himself wrote a 1979 stage adaptation. Despite being a cautionary tale about censorship, the novel itself has been repeatedly challenged and banned in schools. Bradbury always insisted the novel was about television destroying interest in literature, not about government censorship.

questions.question: Who wrote 'Dune'?
questions.explanation: Published in 1965, Dune is an epic science fiction novel set in the distant future on the desert planet Arrakis, following young nobleman Paul Atreides as he is thrust into political intrigue, religious prophecy, and guerrilla warfare after his family assumes control of the galaxy's sole source of melange, a life-extending spice, while the ruthless Baron Vladimir Harkonnen plots their destruction. It won both the Hugo Award (tied) and the inaugural Nebula Award for Best Novel in 1966. Herbert spent six years researching desert ecology before writing the novel, inspired by the Oregon Dunes and U.S. government sand stabilization efforts there. The Bene Gesserit litany, 'Fear is the mind-killer,' has become one of science fiction's most quoted passages. The novel was rejected by 23 publishers before Chilton Books, an automotive manual publisher, took a chance on it. David Lynch directed a controversial 1984 film, and a 2000 Syfy miniseries followed. Denis Villeneuve's 2021 film was nominated for ten Academy Awards and won six, with the 2024 sequel Dune: Part Two completing the story. It is the best-selling science fiction novel of all time.

questions.question: Who wrote 'The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy'?
questions.explanation: Published in 1979, The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy is a comic science fiction novel following Arthur Dent, an ordinary Englishman who survives Earth's destruction (to make way for a hyperspace bypass) by hitchhiking onto an alien spaceship with his friend Ford Prefect, a researcher for the titular interstellar travel guide, and discovers that the answer to the Ultimate Question of Life, the Universe, and Everything is the number 42. Adams reportedly conceived the idea while lying drunk in a field in Innsbruck, Austria, holding a copy of the Hitch-hiker's Guide to Europe. The story originated as a 1978 BBC Radio series before becoming a novel, selling 250,000 copies in its first three months. The cover of the Guide bears the iconic reassurance 'DON'T PANIC.' It ranked #4 in the BBC's 2003 Big Read poll. The answer '42' has become so culturally embedded that it appears throughout technology and pop culture; Elon Musk's Tesla Roadster displayed 'DON'T PANIC' on its dashboard when launched into space in 2018. Despite being called a 'trilogy,' the series ultimately contained five novels, with a sixth by Eoin Colfer commissioned after Adams's sudden death from a heart attack in 2001 at age 49. The 2005 film featured Martin Freeman as Arthur Dent.

questions.question: Who wrote 'The Name of the Rose'?
questions.explanation: Published in 1980, The Name of the Rose is a postmodern historical mystery set in a Northern Italian Benedictine abbey in November 1327, following Franciscan friar William of Baskerville (whose name alludes to Sherlock Holmes) as he investigates a series of murders that revolve around a poisoned book purportedly containing Aristotle's lost second treatise on comedy, guarded by the elderly blind librarian Jorge of Burgos (an homage to Jorge Luis Borges). Eco was already a famous academic semiotician and professor of semiotics at the University of Bologna when he wrote his first novel, proving that intellectually demanding fiction could be a global bestseller. It sold over 50 million copies worldwide, won the Strega Prize in 1981 and the Prix Medicis Etranger in 1982, and ranks #14 on Le Monde's 100 Books of the Century. Eco chose the title somewhat randomly, wanting 'a totally neutral title' and selecting it from suggestions offered by friends. The novel contains intentional historical anachronisms like bell peppers and violins that Eco acknowledged, attributing them to the fictional manuscript's unreliable medieval narrator. Sean Connery starred in the celebrated 1986 Jean-Jacques Annaud film, and a 2019 miniseries featured John Turturro.

questions.question: Who wrote 'A Passage to India'?
questions.explanation: Published in 1924, A Passage to India is a modernist novel set in the fictional city of Chandrapore in British India during the 1910s-1920s, following the friendship between Dr. Aziz, a young Muslim Indian physician, and Cyril Fielding, a liberal British college principal, which is shattered when a British woman accuses Dr. Aziz of assault during an expedition to the Marabar Caves, triggering a trial that exposes the racial tensions of the British Raj. It won the 1924 James Tait Black Memorial Prize and is ranked among the Modern Library's 100 great works of 20th-century English literature and Time magazine's All-Time 100 Novels. The title derives from Walt Whitman's 1870 poem 'Passage to India' from Leaves of Grass. It was Forster's last novel -- remarkably, he lived another 46 years until 1970 but never published another, making it one of the most famous cases of literary silence. The manuscript sold in 1960 for 6,500 pounds, a record price for a modern English manuscript at the time. David Lean directed the acclaimed 1984 film adaptation, which won two Academy Awards. Forster based the fictional Marabar Caves on the real Barabar Caves of Bihar, India.

questions.question: Who wrote 'Native Son'?
questions.explanation: Published in 1940, Native Son is a social protest novel set on Chicago's South Side during the 1930s, following 20-year-old Bigger Thomas, a Black youth trapped in poverty who accidentally kills a white woman and faces a racially charged trial in a system designed against him. It sold 250,000 hardcover copies within three weeks, and Irving Howe declared 'the day Native Son appeared, American culture was changed forever.' It was the first book by an African American author selected by the Book of the Month Club, though the Club edition omitted a masturbation scene and other sexually suggestive content; the complete restored text was not published until 1991. Wright wrote it in just four months. Orson Welles directed a 1941 stage adaptation starring Canada Lee, and film versions appeared in 1951 (an Argentine production in which Wright himself played Bigger), 1986, and 2019 directed by Rashid Johnson. The title was originally created by Chicago writer Nelson Algren for his own unpublished work, but he gave it to Wright. The novel established Wright as what many call the 'father of Black American literature' and directly influenced James Baldwin, Ralph Ellison, and Toni Morrison.

questions.question: Who wrote 'Their Eyes Were Watching God'?
questions.explanation: Published in 1937, Their Eyes Were Watching God is a bildungsroman set in Central and Southern Florida during the early 1900s-1930s, following Janie Crawford as she recounts her journey through three marriages -- from the stifling Logan Killicks to the ambitious but domineering Joe Starks to the passionate younger Tea Cake -- searching for love, independence, and authentic selfhood. Hurston wrote the entire novel in just seven weeks while living in Haiti on a Guggenheim Fellowship, drawing on her training as an anthropologist to render Black Southern dialect with unprecedented authenticity. The novel was initially poorly received by the African American literary establishment; Richard Wright dismissed it as lacking social protest, and it fell into obscurity. Its dramatic rediscovery came in the 1970s when Alice Walker championed it in a 1975 Ms. magazine essay titled 'In Search of Zora Neale Hurston,' visiting Hurston's unmarked grave in Fort Pierce, Florida and placing a headstone there. It is now considered a foundational text of both African American and women's literature, included in Time's 100 Best English-Language Novels since 1923. A 2005 television film produced by Oprah Winfrey starred Halle Berry as Janie.

questions.question: Who wrote 'The Bell Jar'?
questions.explanation: Published in January 1963 under the pseudonym Victoria Lucas, The Bell Jar is Sylvia Plath's only novel, a semi-autobiographical work set in New York City and the Boston suburbs during the summer of 1953, following 19-year-old Esther Greenwood, an ambitious English major whose summer internship at a fashion magazine spirals into a devastating depression, suicide attempt, and psychiatric hospitalization that mirrors Plath's own experiences. It was initially rejected by Harper and Row as 'disappointing, juvenile and overwrought' before finding a British publisher. Plath died by suicide just one month after the UK publication, at age 30, and the novel was not published in the United States until 1971. It has since become a classic of feminist literature and one of the most vivid depictions of mental illness in fiction, with the bell jar metaphor -- the suffocating glass enclosure of depression -- becoming widely used in psychological discourse. The pseudonym was meant to protect the feelings of people depicted in the novel, many of whom were recognizable figures from Plath's life. A 1979 film starred Marilyn Hassett. A new adaptation directed by Sarah Polley and starring Billie Eilish was announced in March 2026.

questions.question: Who wrote 'The Importance of Being Earnest'?
questions.explanation: Premiering on February 14, 1895 at St James's Theatre in London, The Importance of Being Earnest is a farcical comedy of manners set in Mayfair and a Hertfordshire country house, following two young men, Jack Worthing and Algernon Moncrieff, who lead double lives under the assumed name Ernest to evade social obligations while pursuing romantic interests, all under the imperious gaze of the magnificently formidable Lady Bracknell. The pun in the title -- 'Earnest' (sincere) versus 'Ernest' (the name) -- drives the entire plot. By 1995 it was described as 'the second most known and quoted play in English after Hamlet.' The Marquess of Queensberry, father of Wilde's lover Lord Alfred Douglas, planned to disrupt the premiere by throwing rotten vegetables at the stage, but police barred his entrance and he left his vegetable bouquet at the stage door instead. Wilde originally wrote a four-act version, but director George Alexander demanded it be cut to three acts, excising an entire character called Mr. Gribsby. Wilde was arrested just weeks after the play's triumphant opening, ending his career. Notable film adaptations include a 1952 Anthony Asquith version and a 2002 Oliver Parker film.

questions.question: Who wrote 'The Crying of Lot 49'?
questions.explanation: Published in 1966, The Crying of Lot 49 is a postmodern novella set in mid-1960s Northern California, following Oedipa Maas, a suburban housewife who is named executor of her ex-lover's estate and gradually uncovers cryptic symbols suggesting a centuries-old underground postal conspiracy called the Tristero (or Trystero), though whether the conspiracy is real or a product of her paranoia is never resolved. Pynchon is one of literature's great recluses -- no verified photograph of him has been published since the 1950s, making him one of the most private major authors alive. Despite packing conspiracy, entropy, paranoia, and communication theory into just 150 pages, it was included on Time magazine's list of the 100 best English-language novels since 1923. Pynchon himself was critical of the novel, writing in his 1984 collection Slow Learner that he seemed to have 'forgotten most of what I thought I'd learned' while composing it. Radiohead named their official merchandise operation and mailing list W.A.S.T.E. after the novel's secret postal service acronym ('We Await Silent Tristero's Empire'). The novel features a play-within-a-novel called The Courier's Tragedy and is considered a foundational text of postmodern American fiction.
